docs.title: New Features
docs.text:
• Folder Tracking & Dynamic Tags: You can now set up auto-tracking for specific folders. You also have the option to set up rules that automatically bulk-apply specific tags to both existing and newly created markdown notes within those folders.
  • Sidebar Progress Toggle: Added a new switch in the settings so you can independently show or hide the reading progress indicator in the review sidebar.
  • File Path Tooltips: Hovering over a note in the sidebar can now display its relative path within your vault. You can toggle this feature on or off and customize the hover delay in your settings.

docs.title: Improvements
docs.text:
• Smoother Folder Tracking UI: Polished the folder tracking menu with better text, improved spacing, and a more intuitive workflow for setting up auto-tags.
  • Refined "Safe Truncation" Settings: The settings panel for handling long-context truncation is now much clearer and more reliable to configure.
  • Cleaner Settings Menu: Removed the overly technical object-level debug tracking. This removes unnecessary clutter from your settings panel and keeps the plugin running lean.
  • Better Documentation: Updated and reorganized both the English and Chinese README files so the plugin's structure and instructions are much easier to follow.

docs.title: Bug Fixes
docs.text:
• Tracking Logic Priority: Fixed an issue with note tracking resolution. Manual tracking will now reliably override folder-based rules, and legacy tag data will no longer interfere with your tracking results.
  • Tooltip Behavior: Fixed the sidebar file path tooltips so they correctly respect your custom hover delay settings before appearing.
  • Folder Tracking UI Tweaks: Squashed several minor visual bugs in the folder tracking menu, including incorrect button text, weird spacing, and minor interaction glitches.
  • Text & Display Consistency: Fixed various text inconsistencies between the sidebar and timeline, including keeping timeline titles in English and removing leftover internal placeholder text from the auto-tag button.

• Kept progress bar styling in settings while making deck options the only visibility toggle for review countdown bars.
  • Wired the review countdown bar to respect the configured color, warning color, height, and fill direction.
  • Released version 0.0.7 with aligned manifest, package, compatibility metadata, and release notes.

• Restored the async review sequencer contract so review actions no longer break the TypeScript test pipeline after the review-flow refactor.
  • Released version 0.0.6 with aligned manifest, package, and compatibility metadata for distribution.

• Fixed the sync progress toast so runtime status text uses locale keys instead of corrupted hard-coded strings.
  • Fixed the review session first-card render path so the front side appears on the initial screen without reopening or switching cards.
  • Added a reviewer-aligned mojibake audit script and kept the release repository file delta limited to the intended tracked additions.

• Removed the default Anki-cloze hotkeys from the shipped command metadata for community review compliance.
  • Replaced the custom folder suggester, legacy trash call, localStorage popover sizing, navigator.platform, and new Function interpolation with review-safe implementations.
  • Moved status bar due styling out of runtime style injection and into static CSS-driven presentation for the public release build.

• Finalized the plugin description for the initial community submission and aligned it across the repo, release metadata, and community submission.
  • Kept the first public release on 0.0.3 while replacing the unpublished short-description draft.

• Marked the release as desktop-only for the first community submission.
  • Removed package-lock duplication and aligned runtime date usage with Obsidian's exported moment.
  • Trimmed noisy production debug logs and moved modal inline styles into shared CSS utilities.

• Reframed the repository and public-facing documentation around the Syro identity.
  • Removed legacy donation links, outdated repository references, and inherited plugin branding from the main entry points.
  • Simplified the documentation site while the Syro docs are being rebuilt.

docs.title: Syro Documentation Overview
docs.text: Note: The screenshots in this documentation are mainly provided as visual anchors. Please rely on the actual interface of the plugin version you currently have installed.

docs.title: Welcome to Syro
docs.text:
Syro is an Obsidian plugin deeply tailored for knowledge scheduling. It seamlessly integrates the scientific principles of Spaced Repetition and Incremental Reading into a plain-text local knowledge base.

This official documentation is designed to help you start from zero and gradually learn how to turn static Markdown notes into dynamic review queues, so you can actively fight forgetting while building deeper contextual connections across your knowledge.

docs.title: Recommended Reading Path
docs.text: To keep the learning curve manageable, we have trimmed and regrouped the documentation around the core workflows. We recommend exploring it in the following order:

docs.title: Part 1: Getting Started
docs.text:
Before diving into hands-on use, understanding the design philosophy behind the tool will significantly improve your efficiency.

  • Core Concepts: Spaced Repetition and Incremental Reading - Understand the distinctive path Syro takes and the concrete problems it is designed to solve.
  • 5-Minute Quick Start - Walk through a minimal closed loop and quickly experience the full workflow from authoring cards to reviewing them.

docs.title: Part 2: Flashcards
docs.text:
Focused on active recall and atomic knowledge testing.

  • Flashcards Overview - Understand the overall architecture of the flashcard module.
  • Elegant Flashcard Authoring - Learn the Markdown syntax rules for Q/A cards and cloze cards.
  • Managing Review and Flow - Learn how to read the deck tree, score review sessions, and control your daily cognitive load.

docs.title: Part 3: Note Review
docs.text:
Focused on the gradual digestion of long-form literature and study material.

  • Note Review Overview - Understand how incremental reading is implemented inside Obsidian.
  • Managing the Note Review Queue - Learn how to track notes, use the sidebar, and reshape reading order through tags and priority.
  • Timeline: Save Reading History and Progress - Understand how the system preserves reading context across time.

docs.title: Part 4: Advanced & FAQ
docs.text:
Focused on protecting your knowledge assets and solving occasional edge-case problems.

  • Data, Sync & Backup - Learn how the underlying JSON files are stored and how to sync them safely across devices.
  • FAQ & Troubleshooting - Follow a standardized self-check checklist when you run into issues such as cards not being parsed or queues not updating.

Appendix:

  • Changelog
  • License

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
As a plugin deeply aligned with Obsidian's local-first philosophy, Syro will never upload your learning data to a closed cloud service. This page explains, transparently, where scheduling data is stored, how the system captures text changes, and how to back it up or migrate it safely.

If you sync across devices, or are curious about how the system remembers review progress, start here.

docs.title: 1. Sync mechanism: how does the system keep up with you?
docs.text:
Obsidian gives users a great deal of freedom in file operations. To make sure review cards stay aligned with their original text, Syro provides a robust sync strategy:

  • Automatic incremental sync: this is the default and recommended mode. When you edit a flashcard, add a new cloze, or change tags, the system silently computes those small differences in the background and updates the review database in real time. Most of the time, you barely notice it.
  • Manual full rebuild (Rebuild Cache): if you batch-edit hundreds of files with external scripts, or pull a large number of files on another device through iCloud, Syncthing, or a similar tool, automatic sync may miss changes. In that case, run Syro: Rebuild Cache from the Command Palette to force a full scan and realignment.

docs.title: 2. Storage model: where is your data?
docs.text:
All of Syro's scheduling state is stored safely as plain-text JSON inside your local plugin directory, specifically under .obsidian/plugins/syro/. The core files include:

  • tracked_files.json: the main data file that stores parsed flashcard results, FSRS scheduling state, and note-tracking state.
  • review_notes.json / review_commits.json: separate files that store Timeline history and reading context for the note-review queue.
  • (You may also see temporary files with the .overlay suffix. These are incremental cache layers used to avoid write conflicts and speed up updates to large files. They are normal.)

docs.title: 3. Backup and multi-device migration
docs.text:
Because all data is stored strictly inside your Obsidian vault:

  • Normal backup: as long as you back up the entire hidden .obsidian folder, all of Syro's learning progress, card ratings, and scheduling state will be preserved.
  • Multi-device use: if you rely on Obsidian Sync or third-party sync tools such as Git or OneDrive, make sure your sync rules do not exclude the JSON files under .obsidian/plugins/syro/. Otherwise, your review progress will not roam between desktop and mobile.

Tip: before making any manual edits to the underlying JSON files, always copy the folder first as a safety backup.

Related chapter:

  • FAQ & Troubleshooting

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
When you work with a complex system built on local text parsing, occasional local anomalies are inevitable. This page summarizes the issues users run into most often and provides a practical self-check checklist for each one.

If you are wondering why cards are not showing up or why data is not updating, use the guides below to narrow the issue down quickly.

docs.title: Symptom 1: I wrote cards or cloze deletions, but they do not appear in the deck tree
docs.text:
In most cases, the parser simply failed to match your Markdown input. Checklist:

  1. Check the separator settings: go to plugin settings -> Flashcards and confirm that the separators you typed (for example ::) exactly match the configured rules. Pay attention to full-width versus half-width punctuation.
  2. Check the source toggles: if you used highlights such as ==text== as a cloze source, make sure the corresponding setting for using highlights as a cloze source is enabled.
  3. Use isolation for debugging: do not hunt for bugs inside a huge, highly formatted note. Create a brand-new empty note and write a single standard test card like A::B. If it parses correctly there, then some more complex formatting in the original note - such as missing blank lines or badly nested code blocks - is likely blocking the parser.

docs.title: Symptom 2: Queue numbers look wrong, or review reports "note not found"
docs.text:
This usually happens after large-scale file moves or folder renames, when cached paths no longer match reality. Checklist:

  1. Wait a few seconds: for small changes, automatic incremental sync often repairs the issue on its own.
  2. Trigger a full rebuild: open the Command Palette and run Syro: Rebuild Cache (Full Parse). This resolves the vast majority of state-versus-text mismatches.
  3. Clean ghost data: if a note has been physically deleted but its cards still keep appearing in review, run Syro: Clean Ghost Cards to remove invalid references.

docs.title: Symptom 3: The underlying review algorithm seems to do nothing
docs.text:
Checklist:

  1. Confirm which algorithm is active: make sure FSRS is actually enabled in settings. Some older compatibility parameters do nothing unless the corresponding legacy mode is active.
  2. Check daily caps: if you feel certain cards should be showing up but never do, open the deck tree, click the gear icon on the relevant deck, and inspect Reviews/Day. If the cap is too low, cards may simply be blocked from surfacing today.

docs.title: Advanced help and feedback
docs.text:
If none of the above resolves the issue:

  • In the plugin settings under the UI tab, enable Verbose Logging, then press Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + I to open the developer tools. The console will print detailed parsing errors that can help you pinpoint the exact line that caused the failure.
  • You are welcome to bring a minimal Markdown snippet that reproduces the issue - after making sure it does not expose private information - together with the relevant logs to the community or the code repository and file an issue.

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
The flashcard module is the core place where Syro applies Active Recall and Spaced Repetition.

Unlike traditional standalone spaced-repetition software, Syro's flashcards are embedded directly inside your Obsidian notes. That means you can perform atomic knowledge tests while still being only one click away from the original Markdown source and its richer context.

This section presents the full workflow loop, from knowledge extraction to structured review.

docs.title: Core Workflow at a Glance
docs.text:
Syro gives you flexible paths into flashcard review, so you can choose the mode that fits your current focus:

  • Global view: run Review flashcards from all notes to open the panoramic deck tree that includes every due card in your vault.
  • Focused review: while reading a specific note, run Review flashcards in this note to review only the cards from the current document.

docs.title: Chapter Navigation
docs.text:
To help you master the flashcard workflow systematically, this section is split into two key parts:

  1. Elegant Flashcard Authoring

    • Learn how to create Q/A and cloze cards with the most natural Markdown markers, such as separators, highlights, and bold text, without breaking your writing flow.
    • Includes notes about support for more complex layouts such as code blocks and LaTeX formulas.

  2. Managing Review and Flow

    • Learn how to interpret the numbers in the deck tree (New, Learn, Due).
    • Learn how to grade cards objectively inside a review session (Again, Hard, Good, Easy) so the scheduling algorithm receives the right signal.
    • Learn how to use deck options to cap your daily limits and control long-term cognitive load.

Next step: If you have not created any cards yet, start with Elegant Flashcard Authoring.

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
Turning notes into testable flashcards is the key step in building a personal spaced-repetition system (SRS). Syro's design philosophy is "frictionless crystallization": you do not need to leave your current writing flow. With the most natural Markdown markers, you can generate cards directly inline.

This page introduces the main card formats supported by Syro. If you want to learn how to turn plain explanatory text into efficient test units, this is the guide to start with.

docs.title: 1. Q/A Cards
docs.text:
Q/A cards are the most classic and most stable flashcard format. They are especially suitable for term definitions, conceptual distinctions, or formula recall.

  • Single-line Q/A: use the default separator ::. 
    What is the Testing Effect?:: It is the phenomenon in which actively retrieving information significantly improves long-term retention.

  • Multi-line Q/A: if the answer is longer or contains lists or images, you can use a multi-line separator (for example ? by default, or a custom one in settings).

    List three core advantages of incremental reading.
?

1. Reduce cognitive fatigue
2. Improve meta-memory judgments
3. Spark cross-disciplinary associations

docs.title: 2. Cloze Deletions
docs.text:
Cloze cards are Syro's signature feature. They let you preserve the richness of the original context while hiding only the core concept.

  • Based on highlights / bold text: As long as the corresponding options are enabled in settings, you can directly use Obsidian's highlight syntax ==text== or bold syntax **text**.

    The mitochondrion is called the ==powerhouse== of the cell because it produces the ATP needed for life.

    (During review, the phrase powerhouse will be hidden and revealed after you answer.)

  • Command shortcuts (multi-level cloze): When a paragraph contains multiple knowledge points worth testing, you can select text and run Syro: Create Cloze (Same Level) or Syro: Create Cloze (New Level) to quickly generate the standard {{c1::text}} format.

docs.title: 3. Advanced Card Formats
docs.text:
For users with more specialized layout needs, Syro also provides strong parsing support:

  • Tables and code blocks: you can place Markdown tables or fenced code blocks directly inside the answer area. Syro will preserve the formatting and render it cleanly during review.
  • LaTeX math formulas: for technical users, Syro fully supports LaTeX formulas wrapped in $$ inside questions, answers, or cloze cards, so formulas display accurately during review.

docs.title: Best-Practice Suggestions
docs.text:
• Atomic principle: one card should ideally test only one core idea. If an answer runs for several hundred words, the card becomes painful to review and difficult to grade objectively.
  • Preserve context: if a card would become ambiguous outside a specific context, place it beneath the relevant note paragraph so you can always jump back to the original source during review.
  • Keep your syntax style consistent: Syro supports highlights, bold text, brace syntax, and more. In a personal vault, it is usually best to settle on one or two authoring patterns that feel natural to you and keep your notes tidy.

Related chapter:

  • Managing Review and Flow

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
Once you have accumulated a large number of flashcards in your vault, the first real challenge is how to distribute your daily review energy scientifically. This page brings together the core logic of the deck tree overview, review-session interaction, and deck-option controls.

If you want to understand how to read the review tasks generated by the system, how to move smoothly during testing, and how to prevent daily review overload, this is the page to follow.

docs.title: 1. The Deck Tree: Your cognitive dashboard
docs.text:
Run Review flashcards from all notes to open the deck tree. Here your cards are grouped by folder or tag, and each node displays counts for three states:

  • New: untouched territory - cards that have not yet been learned.
  • Learn: cards in the fragile phase of short-term memory, so the system surfaces them more frequently today to prevent forgetting.
  • Due: cards that have already entered long-term memory and have reached the critical moment for reinforcement today.

Tip: You can collapse parent decks that do not need your attention, or trigger review from a single note when you want to focus intensely on a specific area.

docs.title: 2. The Review Session: The arena of active recall
docs.text:
Click any deck that contains numbers to enter a review session. In this interface, smooth interaction is crucial for maintaining flow:

  • Show answer: read the front of the card, search for the answer in your head, and then press the Space key.
  • State grading:
    • 1 - Again (Forgot) - you could not recall it at all. The card returns to relearning.
    • 2 - Hard (Difficult) - you recalled it, but only with significant effort.
    • 3 - Good - a normal recall. This is the standard rating you will use most often.
    • 4 - Easy - the answer came out immediately. The system will push the next review much farther out.
  • Return to the source note: if a card feels confusing, use the menu or shortcut to open the original note behind the card and re-read the full context.

docs.title: 3. Deck Options: Control daily cognitive load
docs.text:
Willpower is limited. If a deck produces hundreds of cards every day, frustration quickly follows. By clicking the gear icon on the right side of a deck, you can adjust its deck options:

  • New Cards/Day: controls how many brand-new cards can be introduced each day. Lowering this number is the most effective way to reduce long-term review pressure.
  • Reviews/Day: controls how many due cards can be shown each day. In most cases, it is best to keep this number relatively high so you protect memories that already exist.

docs.title: Keeping data in sync
docs.text:
For normal day-to-day edits, Syro quietly captures changes through automatic incremental sync in the background. But if you perform large-scale renames, move folders around, or delete many files at once, it is wise to run Syro: Rebuild Cache from the Command Palette so the algorithm's view stays perfectly aligned with your actual file state.

Related chapter:

  • Elegant Flashcard Authoring

docs.title: Core Concepts: Spaced Repetition and Incremental Reading
docs.text:
In the practice of digital note-taking and personal knowledge management (PKM), Obsidian has become a powerful platform for building knowledge bases thanks to its local-first design, plain-text foundations, and bidirectional links. Yet as the amount of information in a vault keeps growing, many users run into a familiar problem: once something is captured, it is forgotten. Carefully clipped articles, fleeting inspirations, and deeper lines of thought often sink back into the graph after being written down.

Syro was created to introduce a "time dimension" into Obsidian's otherwise static text graph. By integrating Spaced Repetition and Incremental Reading, two widely validated cognitive techniques, it helps users build a dynamic workflow that can actively schedule review and fight forgetting.

docs.title: Spaced Repetition: The Scientific Basis for Fighting Forgetting
docs.text:
Spaced repetition is not a new technology. It grows out of the Testing Effect and the Spacing Effect in cognitive psychology. By prompting active retrieval near the edge of the forgetting curve, it strengthens long-term memory with remarkable efficiency.

In this field, Anki is undoubtedly one of the most widely used and successful landmark tools. Through extremely atomic card design, it has helped countless learners - especially medical students and language learners - tackle huge bodies of hard knowledge.

docs.title: Why introduce spaced repetition into Obsidian?
docs.text:
Even though standalone spaced-repetition software such as Anki is extraordinarily efficient at memory retrieval, some users run into an "island effect" when working with highly structured personal notes:

  • To optimize test efficiency, traditional flashcards usually strip knowledge points down to their smallest form and remove them from context. During review, that can sometimes break the internal logic between ideas. If you have forgotten the original background, an isolated card can feel confusing.

Syro takes a complementary path: it preserves structure and context in place. In Syro's workflow, your flashcards - whether Q/A or cloze - are embedded directly inside long-form Markdown notes. When you struggle with a card, you do not need to switch tools. You can reopen the surrounding text at any time, read the full paragraph above or below the card, and even jump through backlinks to related topics. This review experience, grounded in the source text itself, helps you avoid rote memorization while rebuilding the deeper connections behind the knowledge.

docs.title: Incremental Reading: Reshaping How Long-Form Material Gets Digested
docs.text:
Incremental Reading is a revolutionary learning method proposed by Piotr Wozniak, the founder of SuperMemo and one of the pioneers of spaced repetition. It extends the logic of spaced repetition beyond card review into the full process of reading and learning.

Its core idea is simple: instead of trying to finish and fully understand a long piece of material in a single pass, you bring it into the system and let the algorithm split it across multiple moments in the future. Over repeated short encounters, you read, highlight, extract, and eventually turn the material into testable cards.

docs.title: How incremental reading works, and why it matters
docs.text:
SuperMemo demonstrated how powerful incremental reading can be when dealing with large amounts of complex information. Syro borrows that idea and provides a foundational implementation inside Obsidian:

  1. Manage cognitive load and sustain learning momentum When facing dozens of pages of dense literature, people easily become intimidated or lose focus. Incremental reading lets you move across multiple topics in parallel. When you grow tired of the current material, you can stop at any time. The algorithm remembers your progress, and you can move seamlessly to the next article that interests you. That kind of on-demand switching helps keep the brain in a more effective state for absorption.
  2. Let meta-memory mature and improve card quality On a first read, we often lack a global view and treat everything as equally important. Incremental reading encourages delayed processing: weeks later, when an excerpt resurfaces in the queue, you are in a better position to judge whether it is truly worth turning into a flashcard. This prevents the system from filling up with large numbers of low-quality, redundant cards.
  3. Spark cross-disciplinary associations Because the review queue is generated dynamically by the algorithm, you may read computer science, history, and psychology on the same day. That high degree of interleaving and unpredictability creates opportunities for the brain to cross disciplinary boundaries and form unexpected semantic links.

docs.title: Syro's Design Vision
docs.text:
Syro is not meant to replace Anki or SuperMemo outright. Those outstanding tools still have unmatched strengths in their own specialized domains, such as very high card throughput or extremely granular incremental decomposition.

Syro's vision is this: inside Obsidian's open, free, plain-text ecosystem, provide knowledge workers with a built-in engine that makes reading, thinking, and remembering flow together naturally.

By combining underlying scheduling algorithms such as FSRS with your everyday writing flow, Syro aims to help you:

  • eliminate the decision fatigue of "what should I read today?" and "what should I review today?"
  • keep high-value notes and ideas resurfacing instead of fading into silence
  • consolidate memory with as little friction as possible while preserving full knowledge context

If you are ready to experience a learning workflow that combines active retrieval with incremental scheduling, continue to the 5-Minute Quick Start.

docs.title: 5-Minute Quick Start
docs.text: Note: This page walks you through the smallest complete Syro workflow. We strongly recommend performing the steps once in your own vault - it is far more effective than reading any long manual.

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text: When a plugin is feature-rich, the best way to learn it is to begin with the simplest possible closed loop. This page shows you how, in roughly five minutes, to bring a normal Obsidian note into the review system and complete your very first active-recall session.

docs.title: Step 1: Track your first note (incremental reading)
docs.text:
If you have an article or note that you want to read over time:

  1. Open a note in Obsidian that you want to study.
  2. Open the Command Palette (usually Ctrl/Cmd + P), search for Syro: Track this note, and run it.
  3. Search for Syro: Open Notes Review Queue in sidebar and run it.
  4. You should now see the note inside the right sidebar under sections such as Today or New. It has successfully entered your incremental-reading queue.

docs.title: Step 2: Create your first flashcard (knowledge extraction)
docs.text:
Now let us extract a specific knowledge point from that note:

  1. Create a Q/A card: in a blank area of the note, type a question, separate it with ::, and then type the answer. Example: What is Obsidian's core philosophy?:: It is a local-first knowledge base built on plain text.
  2. Create a cloze card: highlight an important phrase in the note using Obsidian's built-in syntax ==text==. Example: Syro is a review plugin built on the ==spaced repetition== model.

docs.title: Step 3: Launch your first review session
docs.text:
Once the card is ready, let us test the brain's retrieval ability:

  1. In the Command Palette, run Syro: Review flashcards in this note.
  2. A review screen will appear. You will see the question you just wrote, or the highlighted phrase with its key part hidden.
  3. Recall the answer in your head, then press the Space key or click Show Answer.
  4. Based on how easily you recalled it, click Again, Hard, Good, or Easy.

Congratulations! You have now completed Syro's core loop end to end. The scheduling algorithm has recorded your feedback and arranged the next review time for both the note and the cards you just created.

docs.title: Suggested entry points for daily use
docs.text:
To make everyday use easier, you can quickly enter Syro through the following places:

  • Status Bar: the bottom-right corner of the Obsidian window usually shows the number of notes and flashcards waiting for review. Click it to jump in quickly.
  • Context Menu: right-click a file in the file list, or the current note tab, to quickly track or untrack it.

Explore more workflows:

  • If you want to learn more card-authoring syntax, continue to Elegant Flashcard Authoring.
  • If you want to learn how to manage long-form reading queues, continue to Managing the Note Review Queue.

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
The Note Review module is the core implementation of Syro's Incremental Reading workflow.

Unlike flashcard review, which tests small knowledge units, note review works at the level of entire Markdown files. When you are faced with long articles, read-later pages, or dense study material, it is often unrealistic to digest everything in one sitting. This module helps you break those long-range tasks apart, distribute them intelligently across future fragments of time, and gradually complete understanding and extraction through repeated passes.

docs.title: Core Workflow at a Glance
docs.text:
To implement incremental reading inside Obsidian, Syro provides a toolset built around the ideas of a queue and a preserved context:

  • Build a reading queue: run the Track action on notes to hand them over to the underlying scheduling algorithm, such as WMS.
  • Allocate reading energy: use the review-queue sidebar on the right, where the system ranks the notes most worth reading today.
  • Preserve reading state: with the Timeline mechanism, the system records your scroll position when you interrupt reading so the next session can resume seamlessly.

docs.title: Chapter Navigation
docs.text:
To help you build orderly long-form reading habits, continue with the following detailed guides:

  1. Managing the Note Review Queue

    • Learn how to add single notes or entire folders to the review system and remove them when needed.
    • Learn how to interpret the time-based groups in the sidebar interface (Today, Overdue, Future, and so on).
    • Learn how to influence the algorithm's ordering through tag filtering and priority so your most important domains always stay near the top.

  2. Timeline: Save Reading Position and History

    • Learn how the system captures the exact place where each reading session stops.
    • Learn how to use the history drawer to quickly recover earlier reading context and reduce the cognitive cost of task switching.

Next step: If you want to bring your first article into the scheduling system right away, start with Managing the Note Review Queue.

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
The power of incremental reading lies in the system's ability to pull out the few pieces that are most worth reading today from a sea of material. This page explains how to track notes, how to read the queue sidebar on the right, and how to reshape your reading order through tags and priority.

If you need to distribute attention across multiple long texts, or want to pin material from a particular domain near the top, this guide is the place to start.

docs.title: 1. Add material to the schedule, and remove it again
docs.text:
The system does not automatically grab every file in your vault. You must explicitly authorize it through the Track action:

  • Track in place: while reading a note, run Syro: Track this note, or choose the equivalent right-click action.
  • Track in bulk: right-click a folder in the file tree and choose the action that tracks every note inside that directory.
  • If a note is no longer worth keeping in the schedule, run Untrack to remove it from the queue. This never deletes the underlying local file.

docs.title: 2. Read the queue sidebar
docs.text:
Run Syro: Open Notes Review Queue in sidebar to open the queue interface. The algorithm groups candidate notes into collapsible time-based sections:

  • New: newly tracked material that has not been meaningfully read yet.
  • Overdue: tasks that were missed or set aside earlier. There is no need to panic - you can pick them up again at any time.
  • Today: the notes the algorithm believes are most suitable for you to revisit today.
  • (Click any item in the sidebar to open the original note in the main editor and continue reading.)

docs.title: 3. Shape your reading focus with tags and priority
docs.text:
When your queue grows too large, you can influence the algorithm's ordering in two ways:

  • Tag filtering: Select a specific tag in the filter bar at the top of the sidebar (for example #psychology) and the queue instantly narrows down to material in that topic. You can also configure Ignored Tags in the plugin settings so notes with tags such as #draft are permanently excluded from view.

  • Priority: When several notes are due on the same day, which one should surface first? Click the priority-edit button on a note item and assign it a higher number, such as 80 or 100. High-priority notes gain the algorithmic privilege of cutting the line, ensuring that high-value material captures your attention first.

(Important: after you finish reading, remember to perform a Review or Advance action on the note. This removes it from today's queue and lets the algorithm assign the next revisit date.)

Related chapter:

  • Timeline: Save Reading Position and History

docs.title: Timeline: Save Reading Position and History
docs.text: Note: Some screenshots in this documentation may come from earlier versions of the interface, but the core layout and entry logic remain largely the same and can still serve as a useful visual reference.

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
The essence of incremental reading is to spread long material across multiple moments in time. Timeline is the context-recovery tool built specifically for that purpose. It records when you previously reviewed a note and, whenever possible, helps you return to roughly the same scroll position where you stopped reading last time.

If you often switch between multiple heavy tutorials or book excerpts and are tired of reopening a note only to search for where you left off, this page is for you.

docs.title: What Timeline does
docs.text:
Without Timeline, interrupting a reading session often means losing context. By recording both reading progress (scroll percentage) and historical commit states, Timeline dramatically reduces the cognitive cost of switching tasks.

With this mechanism, you can confidently split a ten-thousand-word article into ten short ten-minute reading sessions. The system keeps the anchor for every pause on your behalf.

docs.title: How do you view and use Timeline?
docs.text:
1. Expand the timeline drawer:
    • In the review-queue sidebar, select and open a tracked note.
    • Beneath that note item - or through the relevant quick action - a Timeline drawer can usually be expanded.
  2. Interpret the history:
    • Inside the drawer, you will see each historical commit for that note, including the timestamp and the approximate place reached in the document (for example 45%).
  3. Restore context:
    • Click an older entry in the Timeline and the main editor will attempt to scroll back to the approximate position from that reading session. This makes it far easier to recover the surrounding context.

docs.title: When does Timeline record a new entry?
docs.text:
Timeline is not a high-frequency real-time log. It usually records state only when you perform an explicit review action on a note.

  • After reading part of a note, if you run a Review, Good, Hard, or postponement action through the command or the UI, the system captures the current scroll position and stores it as a new Timeline record.

docs.title: Interface customization and settings
docs.text:
If Timeline takes up too much sidebar space, or you do not need scroll-position history, you can adjust it in the plugin settings under the Notes tab:

  • Show scroll percentage: show or hide the numeric progress display.
  • Auto-expand Timeline: decide whether clicking a note automatically expands its Timeline drawer.

docs.title: Common misconceptions
docs.text:
• Expecting pixel-perfect restoration: Timeline relies on document structure and scroll ratios. If you heavily rewrite the source note between two reading sessions, older records may shift and can only be treated as approximate references.
  • Underestimating cleanup risk: Timeline history depends on underlying data files such as review_commits.json. If you delete those files while troubleshooting, the note's historical reading context will disappear as well.

Related chapters:

  • Managing the Note Review Queue
  • Data, Sync & Backup
  • Note Review Overview

docs.title: Syro 中文文档总览
docs.text: 注：文档中的截图主要用于辅助视觉定位，具体界面请以您当前安装的插件版本为准。

docs.title: 欢迎来到 Syro
docs.text:
Syro 是一款为 Obsidian 深度定制的知识调度插件。它将间隔重复（Spaced Repetition）与渐进阅读（Incremental Reading）的科学理念无缝融入纯文本的本地知识库中。

这份官方文档旨在帮助您从零开始，逐步掌握如何将静态的 Markdown 笔记转化为动态的复习队列，从而在知识积累的过程中有效对抗遗忘，建立深层的上下文联系。

docs.title: 推荐阅读路径
docs.text: 为了降低学习成本，我们已将文档按核心工作流进行了精简与重组。建议您按照以下模块顺序进行探索：

docs.title: 📦 第一部分：新手起步 (Getting Started)
docs.text:
在开始动手操作前，了解工具背后的设计哲学将极大提升您的使用效率。

  • 核心理念：间隔重复与渐进阅读 —— 了解 Syro 选择的独特路径及其解决的具体痛点。
  • 5 分钟快速上手指南 —— 通过一个极简的闭环，快速体验制卡与复习的全流程。

docs.title: 🧠 第二部分：闪卡复习 (Flashcards)
docs.text:
专注于知识点的“主动回忆”与原子化测试。

  • 闪卡复习总览 —— 了解闪卡模块的整体架构。
  • 优雅地编写闪卡 —— 掌握问答卡（Q/A）与填空卡（Cloze）的 Markdown 语法规则。
  • 管理复习与心流 —— 学习如何解读牌组树、进行会话评分，以及控制每日的认知负荷。

docs.title: 📖 第三部分：笔记复习 (Note Review)
docs.text:
专注于长篇文献与学习材料的“渐进式消化”。

  • 笔记复习总览 —— 了解渐进阅读在 Obsidian 中的具体落地方式。
  • 掌控笔记复习队列 —— 学习如何追踪笔记、使用侧边栏，以及通过标签和优先级重塑阅读秩序。
  • Timeline：保存阅读历史与进度 —— 了解系统如何为您保留跨越时间的阅读上下文。

docs.title: ⚙️ 第四部分：进阶与数据管理 (Advanced & FAQ)
docs.text:
为您的知识资产提供安全保障，并解答偶发的疑难杂症。

  • 数据、同步与备份机制 —— 了解底层 JSON 文件的存储逻辑与安全的跨端同步方案。
  • 常见问题与排障指南 —— 遇到“卡片未解析”、“队列不更新”等问题时的标准化自检清单。

附录：

  • 更新记录 (Changelog)
  • 开源许可证 (License)

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text:
作为一款深度融合于 Obsidian 本地化理念的插件，Syro 绝不会将您的学习数据上传至任何闭源云端。本页旨在透明地向您解释调度数据存储在哪里、系统如何捕捉您的文本修改，以及如何安全地进行备份与迁移。

如果您在进行跨设备同步，或者对系统“如何记住复习进度”感到好奇，请参考本页内容。

docs.title: 1. 同步机制：系统如何懂你？
docs.text:
Obsidian 具有极高的文件操作自由度。为了确保复习卡片与原始文本不脱节，Syro 提供了一套稳健的同步方案：

  • 自动增量同步（Auto-incremental Sync）：这是默认且推荐的模式。当您修改一张闪卡的内容、添加一个新的填空或更改标签时，系统会在后台静默计算这些微小的差异，并实时更新复习数据库。您几乎感觉不到它的存在。
  • 手动全量重建（Rebuild Cache）：当您使用外部脚本批量修改了数百个文件，或使用第三方同步盘（如 iCloud、Syncthing）在另一台电脑上拉取了大量新文件后，自动同步可能会遗漏。此时，通过命令面板执行 Syro: Rebuild Cache，可强制系统全量扫描并对齐最新状态。

docs.title: 2. 存储模型：您的数据在哪儿？
docs.text:
Syro 的所有调度状态数据，均以纯文本 JSON 格式安全地存储在您的本地插件目录中（具体路径为：.obsidian/plugins/syro/ 下）。 核心文件包括：

  • tracked_files.json：承载所有闪卡的解析结果、FSRS 算法调度状态以及笔记追踪状态的主数据文件。
  • review_notes.json / review_commits.json：独立存储笔记复习队列的 Timeline 历史进度与阅读上下文。
  • (您可能会看到带有 .overlay 后缀的临时文件，这是系统为了防止数据写入冲突和优化大文件写入速度而生成的增量缓存层，属于正常现象。)

docs.title: 3. 备份与多端迁移建议
docs.text:
由于所有数据都严格存储在您的 Obsidian Vault（库）内：

  • 常规备份：只要您正常备份了整个 .obsidian 隐藏文件夹，Syro 的所有学习进度、卡片评分和算法状态都会被完整保留。
  • 跨设备多端使用：如果您使用官方的 Obsidian Sync 或第三方同步工具（如 Git、OneDrive），请务必确保同步规则不要忽略 .obsidian/plugins/syro/ 目录下的 .json 数据文件。否则您的复习进度将无法在手机和电脑间漫游。

提示：在进行任何手动修改底层 JSON 数据文件的高危操作前，请务必先复制一份该文件夹作为安全备份。

相关章节导航：

  • 常见问题与排障指南

docs.title: 常见问题与排障指南 (FAQ & Troubleshooting)
模块导读
docs.text:
在使用基于本地文本解析的复杂系统中，偶尔会遇到由于格式不规范或同步延迟导致的局部异常。本页汇总了用户最常遇到的几类现象及对应的自我排查清单。

如果您遇到“卡片出不来”、“数据不更新”等疑惑，请尝试按照以下指南进行快速定位。

docs.title: 现象 1：我写了卡片/挖空，但在牌组树里看不到
docs.text:
这通常是由于 Markdown 解析规则未能匹配您的输入导致的。 排查清单：

  1. 检查分隔符设置：前往插件设置 -> Flashcards 标签，确认您输入的分隔符（如 ::）与设置中定义的规则完全一致（注意中英文冒号的区别）。
  2. 检查来源开关：如果您使用了高亮 ==文本== 作为挖空，请确保设置中已明确开启“将高亮作为 Cloze 来源”的开关。
  3. 隔离排查法：不要在几千字、排版极其复杂的笔记里找 Bug。请新建一个空白笔记，只写一行最简单的标准问答测试（如 A::B），看能否正常解析。如果可以，说明是您原笔记中的某处复杂排版（如缺少空行、代码块嵌套错误）阻断了解析器。

docs.title: 现象 2：队列数字异常，或复习时报错“找不到笔记”
docs.text:
这通常发生在您大规模移动了文件、重命名了文件夹，导致系统缓存的数据路径失效。 排查清单：

  1. 快速等待（冷笑话）：如果是少量修改，等待几秒钟，自动增量同步会修复它。
  2. 触发全量重建：打开命令面板，执行 Syro: Rebuild Cache (Full Parse)。这通常能解决 95% 的“状态与文本不一致”问题。
  3. 清理幽灵数据：如果某篇笔记已被物理删除，但复习界面依然固执地弹出它的卡片，可执行命令 Syro: Clean Ghost Cards 强制清理无效引用。

docs.title: 现象 3：感觉底层的复习算法不起作用
docs.text:
排查清单：

  1. 新旧算法交替：请确认您在设置中启用的是 FSRS 算法。部分带有旧版兼容标签的参数滑块，若未经激活，调整它们并不会产生实际效果。
  2. 上限拦截：如果您感觉某些该复习的卡片一直不出现，请前往牌组树点击对应牌组的齿轮（⚙️）图标，检查“每日复习上限（Reviews/Day）”是否设置得过低，导致卡片被系统拦截在了今天的大门外。

docs.title: 进阶帮助与反馈
docs.text:
如果您穷尽了以上步骤依然无法解决问题：

  • 您可以在插件设置的 UI 标签中开启 调试模式（Verbose Logging），此时按 Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + I 打开开发者工具，控制台会输出详细的解析错误日志，帮助您锁定具体是哪一行文本引发了崩溃。
  • 欢迎携带有问题的且确保它将不会暴露您的隐私的“最小 Markdown 文本片段”以及您的错误日志，前往社区或代码仓库提交 Issue 寻求支持。

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text:
闪卡（Flashcards）复习模块是 Syro 应用“主动回忆（Active Recall）”与“间隔重复（Spaced Repetition）”机制的核心功能区。

与传统的独立间隔重复软件不同，Syro 的闪卡直接内嵌于您的 Obsidian 笔记中。这意味着您在进行原子化知识测试的同时，随时可以一键回溯到原始的 Markdown 文本中，重新获取丰富的上下文支持。

本组文档将为您完整呈现从“知识提取”到“规律复习”的工作流闭环。

docs.title: 核心工作流概览
docs.text:
Syro 为闪卡复习提供了高度灵活的操作路径，您可以根据当前的专注目标自由选择：

  • 全局视野：通过执行 Review flashcards from all notes，打开包含库中所有待复习卡片的“全景牌组树”。
  • 聚焦当下：在阅读某篇特定笔记时，执行 Review flashcards in this note，系统将仅针对当前文档中的卡片启动沉浸式测试。

docs.title: 章节导航
docs.text:
为帮助您系统掌握闪卡工作流，我们将其拆分为以下两个核心部分：

  1. 优雅地编写闪卡 (Card Authoring)

    • 介绍如何在不打断写作心流的前提下，使用分隔符、高亮、粗体等最自然的 Markdown 标记生成问答卡与填空卡（Cloze）。
    • 包含针对复杂排版（如代码块、LaTeX 公式）的支持说明。

  2. 管理复习与心流 (Review Workflow)

    • 介绍如何解读“牌组树”上的各项数字（New / Learn / Due）。
    • 说明在复习会话中如何进行客观的状态评分（Again / Hard / Good / Easy），以正确引导底层调度算法。
    • 指导您如何通过“牌组选项”设置每日上限，科学控制长期的认知负荷。

下一步： 如果您尚未在笔记中创建任何卡片，建议从 优雅地编写闪卡 开始阅读。

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text:
将笔记转化为可测试的闪卡，是构建个人间隔重复系统（SRS）的关键一步。Syro 的设计哲学是“零阻力结晶”——您不需要离开当前的写作心流，只需使用最自然的 Markdown 标记，即可在行内瞬间生成卡片。

本页将向您介绍 Syro 支持的几种主要卡片格式。如果您想了解如何将平铺直叙的文本转化为高效的测试单元，请参考本指南。

docs.title: 1. 问答卡 (Q/A Cards)
docs.text:
问答卡是最经典、最稳定的闪卡形式，非常适合用于名词解释、概念辨析或公式背诵。

  • 单行问答：使用默认的分隔符 ::。 
    什么是测试效应（Testing Effect）？:: 指通过主动提取记忆来显著提升长期保持率的现象。

  • 多行问答：如果答案较长或包含列表、图片，可以使用多行分隔符（默认如 ?，或可在设置中自定义）。

    列举渐进阅读的三个核心优势？
?

1. 消除认知疲劳
2. 提升元记忆判断
3. 激发跨学科联想

docs.title: 2. 填空卡 (Cloze Deletions)
docs.text:
填空卡（也称挖空卡）是 Syro 核心功能。它允许您保留原文的丰富上下文，仅将核心概念遮挡。

  • 基于高亮 / 粗体： 只要在设置中开启了对应选项，您可以直接使用 Obsidian 的高亮 ==文本== 或粗体 **文本** 语法。

    线粒体被称为细胞的==动力工厂==，它负责产生生命活动所需的 ATP。

    (复习时，这张卡片的“动力工厂”将被遮挡，并在您点击后显示。)

  • 命令快捷键（多级挖空）： 当一段话中有多个需要测试的知识点时，您可以选中文本，通过命令 Syro: Create Cloze (Same Level)（同级挖空）或 Syro: Create Cloze (New Level)（新级挖空）快速生成标准的 {{c1::文本}} 格式。

docs.title: 3. 进阶卡片格式
docs.text:
对于有特殊排版需求的用户，Syro 同样提供了强大的解析支持：

  • 表格与代码块：您可以直接在答案区使用 Markdown 表格或代码块，系统会完整保留其格式并在复习界面中优雅渲染。
  • LaTeX 数学公式：对于理工科用户，Syro 完整支持在问题、答案或挖空中嵌套 $$ 包裹的 LaTeX 公式，确保公式在复习时的精准显示。

docs.title: 最佳实践建议
docs.text:
• 原子化原则（Atomic）：一张卡片最好只测试一个核心概念。如果一张卡片的答案长达两三百字，您在复习时会感到极其痛苦且难以客观评分。
  • 保留上下文：如果卡片脱离了特定语境容易引起歧义，建议将卡片写在相关的笔记段落下方，复习时可随时“一键返回原文”查看背景。
  • 统一语法风格：虽然系统支持高亮、粗体、花括号等多种语法，建议您在个人的知识库中固定使用 1-2 种最顺手的标记，以保持笔记的整洁。

相关章节导航：

  • 管理复习与心流

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text:
当您在库中积累了大量闪卡后，如何科学地调配每天的复习精力便成了首要问题。本页整合了牌组树总览、复习会话操作以及牌组选项控制的核心逻辑。

如果您希望了解如何解读系统给出的复习任务、如何在测试时顺畅操作，以及如何防止每天的复习量过载，请参考本页内容。

docs.title: 1. 牌组树：您的认知仪表盘
docs.text:
通过命令 Review flashcards from all notes，您可以唤出牌组树（Deck Tree）。在这里，您的卡片按目录或标签被归类，并展示三种状态的数字：

  • New（新卡）：待开拓的处女地，尚未开始学习。
  • Learn（学习中）：处于短期记忆脆弱期，系统会在今天高频推送以防遗忘。
  • Due（待复习）：已进入长期记忆，且在今天恰好达到遗忘临界点，亟需巩固。

提示：您可以随时折叠不需要关注的父级牌组，或者通过单篇笔记触发复习，将注意力极度聚焦在特定领域。

docs.title: 2. 复习会话：主动回忆的竞技场
docs.text:
点击任意包含数字的牌组即可进入复习会话。在这个界面中，流畅的交互是保持心流的关键：

  • 显示答案：阅读卡片正面并在脑海中检索答案后，按下 空格键。
  • 状态评分：
    • 1 - Again (忘记)：完全想不起来。卡片将退回重学。
    • 2 - Hard (困难)：费了很大力气才回忆起。
    • 3 - Good (良好)：正常回忆。这是最常用的标准反馈。
    • 4 - Easy (简单)：脱口而出。系统将大幅推迟下次复习。
  • 回溯原文：若对卡片产生疑惑，可通过菜单或快捷键一键在后台打开该卡片所在的原始笔记，重温完整语境。

docs.title: 3. 牌组选项：控制每日认知负荷
docs.text:
人的意志力是有限的。如果某个牌组每天涌现几百张卡片，很容易引发挫败感。通过点击牌组右侧的齿轮（⚙️）图标，您可以调整牌组选项：

  • 每日新卡上限（New Cards/Day）：控制每天最多引入多少张尚未学习的新卡。调低此数值是缓解长期复习压力的最有效手段。
  • 每日复习上限（Reviews/Day）：控制每天最多展示多少张待复习的旧卡。通常建议保持较高数值，以优先捍卫已有记忆。

docs.title: 维持数据同步
docs.text:
日常的少量笔记修改，Syro 会通过后台的自动增量同步静默捕获。但如果您进行了大规模的重命名、移动文件夹或大批量删除操作，建议在命令面板执行一次 Syro: Rebuild Cache（重建缓存），以确保算法的视角与您实际的文件状态绝对对齐。

相关章节导航：

  • 优雅地编写闪卡

docs.title: 核心理念：间隔重复与渐进阅读
docs.text:
在数字笔记与个人知识管理（PKM）的实践中，Obsidian 凭借其本地化、纯文本与双向链接的特性，成为了构建知识库的强大平台。然而，随着库中记录的信息日益庞大，许多用户面临着“记录后即遗忘”的困境——那些精心剪藏的文章、瞬间的灵感或深度的思考，往往在写入后便沉寂于图谱之中。

Syro 插件的开发初衷，正是为了在 Obsidian 静态的文本图谱中引入“时间维度”。它通过整合间隔重复（Spaced Repetition）与渐进阅读（Incremental Reading）这两项被广泛验证的认知技术，协助用户建立一套能够主动调度复习、对抗遗忘的动态工作流。

docs.title: 间隔重复：对抗遗忘的科学基础
docs.text:
间隔重复并非一项新技术。它基于认知心理学中的“测试效应（Testing Effect）”与“间隔效应（Spacing Effect）”，通过在遗忘曲线的临界点进行主动提取练习，以极高的效率巩固长期记忆。

在这个领域，Anki 无疑是目前应用最广泛、最成功的里程碑式工具。它通过极致的原子化卡片设计，帮助无数学习者（尤其是医学生、语言学习者）攻克了海量的硬核知识。

docs.title: 为什么在 Obsidian 中引入间隔重复？
docs.text:
尽管传统的独立间隔重复软件（如 Anki）在记忆提取上效率极高，但在处理高度结构化的个人笔记时，部分用户可能会遇到“孤岛效应”的困扰：

  • 传统闪卡为了优化测试效率，通常要求将知识点极度提炼、剥离语境。这在复习时，有时会导致卡片失去内在的逻辑联系。如果复习者遗忘了当时的背景，面对一张孤立的卡片容易感到困惑。

Syro 选择了一条互补的路径：就地为卡片保留结构与上下文。 在 Syro 的工作流中，您的闪卡（问答或填空）是直接内嵌于长篇 Markdown 笔记中的。当您在复习某张卡片遇到困难时，无需切换软件，您可以随时展开原文，阅读卡片上方或下方的完整段落，甚至通过双向链接跳转到相关主题。这种基于原文原境的复习体验，能够在避免死记硬背的同时，帮助您在更深层次上重建知识的联系。

docs.title: 渐进阅读：重塑长篇材料的消化过程
docs.text:
渐进阅读（Incremental Reading）是由 Piotr Woźniak（SuperMemo 的创始人、间隔重复领域的先驱）提出的一种革命性学习方法。它将间隔重复的理念从“卡片复习”延伸到了“阅读与学习”的全过程。

其核心思想是：不追求一次性读完并理解一篇长篇材料，而是将文章导入系统，依靠算法将其拆解并分散到未来的多个时间点。在每一次短暂的接触中，读者逐步完成阅读、高亮、提炼，并最终将其转化为可测试的卡片。

docs.title: 渐进阅读的机制与优势
docs.text:
SuperMemo 证明了渐进阅读在处理海量复杂信息时的巨大威力。Syro 借鉴了这一理念，并在 Obsidian 中提供了基础的实现：

  1. 管理认知负荷，维持学习状态 面对几十页的晦涩文献，人类极易产生畏难情绪和注意力涣散。渐进阅读允许您同时在多个主题间交错阅读。当对当前材料感到疲倦时，您可以随时中断，算法会记录您的进度，您可以无缝切换到下一篇感兴趣的文章。这种按需切换有助于维持大脑的最佳吸收状态。
  2. 沉淀元记忆（Meta-memory），提升制卡质量 在初次阅读时，我们往往缺乏全局视角，容易将所有内容都视为重点。渐进阅读主张延迟处理：随着时间的推移和对文章理解的加深，当摘录在几周后再次出现在队列中时，您能更准确地判断它是否真的值得被制成闪卡，从而避免产生大量低质量的冗余卡片。
  3. 激发跨学科联想 由于复习队列是基于算法动态生成的，您可能在同一天内阅读到计算机科学、历史学与心理学的材料。这种高度的交错性与不可预测性，为大脑跨越学科边界、产生意想不到的语义联结提供了可能。

docs.title: Syro 的设计愿景
docs.text:
Syro 并非旨在完全替代 Anki 或 SuperMemo，这些卓越的软件在各自的专业领域（如海量卡片吞吐、极端的渐进拆解）仍具有无可比拟的优势。

Syro 的愿景是：在 Obsidian 开放、自由的纯文本生态中，为知识工作者提供一个流畅的、“阅读-思考-记忆”无缝衔接的内建引擎。

通过将底层的调度算法（如 FSRS）与您的日常写作心流相结合，Syro 希望帮助您：

  • 消除“今天该读什么、该复习什么”的决策疲劳。
  • 确保高价值的笔记与灵感能定期浮现，不再沉寂。
  • 在保留完整知识上下文的前提下，以最低的阻力完成记忆的巩固。

如果您准备好体验这种结合了主动提取与渐进调度的学习方式，请查阅我们的 5 分钟快速上手指南。

docs.title: 5 分钟快速上手指南
docs.text: 注：本页将引导您完成 Syro 的最小工作流体验。我们建议您跟随步骤实际操作一次，这比阅读任何复杂的说明书都更为有效。

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text: 面对一个功能丰富的插件，最好的学习方式是从一个最简单的闭环开始。本页将向您展示如何在 5 分钟内，将一篇普通的 Obsidian 笔记纳入复习系统，并完成您的第一次“主动回忆”测试。

docs.title: 步骤一：追踪您的第一篇笔记（渐进阅读）
docs.text:
如果您有一篇需要长线阅读或复习的文章：

  1. 在 Obsidian 中打开一篇您想要学习的笔记。
  2. 唤出 命令面板（Command Palette）（通常通过 Ctrl/Cmd + P），搜索并执行 Syro: Track this note（追踪当前笔记）。
  3. 搜索并执行 Syro: Open Notes Review Queue in sidebar。
  4. 此时，您将在右侧侧边栏的“Today（今天）”或“New（新笔记）”分区中看到这篇笔记。它已经成功进入了您的渐进阅读队列。

docs.title: 步骤二：制作您的第一张闪卡（知识提取）
docs.text:
让我们在这篇笔记中提取一个具体的知识点：

  1. 制作问答卡（Q/A）：在笔记的空白处，输入一个问题，用 :: 隔开，再输入答案。 示例：Obsidian 的核心理念是什么？:: 是一个本地化、基于纯文本的知识库。
  2. 制作填空卡（Cloze）：选中笔记中的某句重点，使用 Obsidian 自带的高亮快捷键（==文本==）将其包裹。 示例：Syro 是一个基于 ==间隔重复== 算法的复习插件。

docs.title: 步骤三：启动您的第一次复习会话
docs.text:
卡片制作完成后，让我们来测试一下大脑的提取能力：

  1. 在命令面板中，执行 Syro: Review flashcards in this note（复习当前笔记中的闪卡）。
  2. 此时，屏幕上将弹出复习界面。您会看到刚才写下的问题，或被遮挡的高亮词。
  3. 在脑海中回忆答案，然后按下 空格键 或点击“显示答案”。
  4. 根据您回忆的顺畅程度，点击底部的 Again、Hard、Good 或 Easy 按钮。

恭喜！ 您已经完整体验了 Syro 的核心闭环。系统底层的调度算法已经记录了您的反馈，并为您安排了这篇笔记与这几张卡片的下一次复习时间。

docs.title: 常用入口建议
docs.text:
为了后续使用的便利，您可以通过以下方式快速触发 Syro：

  • 状态栏（Status Bar）：在 Obsidian 窗口的最右下角，通常会显示待复习的笔记和闪卡数量，点击即可快速进入。
  • 右键菜单：在文件列表或当前笔记的标签页上点击右键，可以快速执行追踪或取消追踪。

探索更多工作流：

  • 如果您想深入了解更多制卡语法，请查阅 优雅地编写闪卡。
  • 如果您想学习如何管理长篇阅读队列，请查阅 掌控笔记复习队列。

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text:
笔记复习（Note Review）模块是 Syro 应用“渐进阅读（Incremental Reading）”机制的核心载体。

与闪卡复习测试零碎知识点不同，笔记复习的单位是完整的 Markdown 文件。面对海量的长篇文献、待读网页或厚重的学习材料，您往往无法一次性将其消化。本模块的功能旨在帮助您将这些长线任务打散，依靠算法智能地分发到未来的碎片时间中，并在多次的接触中逐步完成理解与提炼。

docs.title: 核心工作流概览
docs.text:
为了在 Obsidian 中实现渐进阅读，Syro 提供了一套围绕“队列”与“上下文”的管理工具：

  • 建立待读队列：通过对笔记执行“追踪（Track）”操作，将其移交至底层的调度算法（如 WMS）接管。
  • 分配阅读精力：利用右侧的复习队列侧边栏，系统会为您排序出今天最值得阅读的笔记。
  • 保留阅读状态：通过特有的 Timeline 机制，系统会在您中断阅读时记录当前的滚动位置，确保下次重温时能无缝衔接。

docs.title: 章节导航
docs.text:
为帮助您建立有序的长篇阅读习惯，请参阅以下详细指南：

  1. 掌控笔记复习队列 (Queue Management)

    • 学习如何将单篇笔记或整个文件夹纳入/移出复习系统。
    • 了解如何解读侧边栏界面的时间分区（今天、逾期、未来等）。
    • 掌握如何通过调整标签过滤与优先级（Priority），干预算法排序，确保核心领域的知识始终被优先推送。

  2. Timeline：保存阅读位置与历史进度

    • 了解系统如何捕捉并记录您每一次阅读中断时的精确位置。
    • 学习如何通过历史时间线抽屉，快速回溯过去的阅读上下文，降低任务切换时的认知损耗。

下一步： 如果您希望立即将第一篇文章纳入调度，建议从 掌控笔记复习队列 开始阅读。

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text:
渐进阅读的魅力在于系统能帮您从浩如烟海的文献中，精准挑出今天最值得阅读的那几篇。本页将介绍如何追踪笔记、如何解读右侧队列侧边栏，以及如何通过标签和优先级重塑您的阅读秩序。

如果您需要在多篇长文中分配阅读精力，或希望将某个特定领域的学习材料置顶，请参考本页指南。

docs.title: 1. 纳入与移出调度体系
docs.text:
系统不会自动抓取您库里的所有文件，必须通过追踪（Track）操作明确授权：

  • 就地追踪：在阅读某篇笔记时，执行命令 Syro: Track this note，或右键选择对应选项。
  • 批量追踪：在左侧文件树中右键点击某个文件夹，选择追踪该目录下的所有笔记。
  • 若某篇材料已失去时效性，执行取消追踪（Untrack）即可将其从调度队列中移除，此操作绝对不会删除您的本地物理文件。

docs.title: 2. 解读队列侧边栏
docs.text:
执行 Syro: Open Notes Review Queue in sidebar 可打开控制台。算法会将待读笔记按时间线进行折叠分组：

  • New（新笔记）：刚追踪且尚未开始阅读的材料。
  • Overdue（逾期）：之前错过或搁置的任务。不必焦虑，您可以随时拾起它们。
  • Today（今天）：算法认为今天最适合您重新温习的笔记。
  • (单击侧边栏中的任意条目，即可在主编辑区打开该笔记的原文进行阅读。)

docs.title: 3. 塑造阅读焦点：标签与优先级
docs.text:
当您的队列积压过多时，可以通过以下两种方式干预算法的排序：

  • 标签筛选（Tags）： 在侧边栏顶部的过滤条中选择特定标签（如 #心理学），队列将瞬间收缩，仅显示该主题下的待读文章。您也可以在插件设置中配置“忽略标签（Ignored Tags）”，将包含如 #草稿 等标签的笔记永久剔除出视图。
  • 调整优先级（Priority）： 同一天到期的笔记，谁排在最前面？在笔记条目上点击优先级修改按钮，赋予其一个更高的数值（如 80 或 100）。高优先级的笔记将获得算法的“插队特权”，确保高价值文献始终优先捕获您的注意力。

(注意：阅读完毕后，请记得对该笔记执行“推进 / Review”操作，系统会将其移出今天的队列，并根据算法安排未来的重温日期。)

相关章节导航：

  • Timeline：保存阅读位置与历史进度

docs.title: Timeline：保存阅读位置与历史进度
docs.text: 注：文档中的截图可能来自较早版本的界面，但核心布局与入口逻辑基本保持一致，供您在操作时作为视觉对照。

docs.title: 模块导读
docs.text:
渐进阅读的精髓在于将长篇材料打散到多个不同的时间点进行消化。Timeline（时间线）功能正是为此设计的上下文恢复工具。它不仅记录了您历史上每一次复习该笔记的时间，还能尽可能地帮您定位到上一次阅读结束时的滚动位置。

如果您经常需要在多篇厚重的教程或书籍摘录之间来回切换，并且厌倦了每次打开文档都要“重新寻找上次读到哪儿”，请参考本页内容。

docs.title: Timeline 的核心作用
docs.text: 在没有 Timeline 的情况下，中断阅读往往意味着上下文的丢失。Timeline 通过记录“阅读进度（滚动百分比）”与“历史提交状态”，极大地降低了任务切换时的认知损耗。 通过这一机制，您可以放心地将一万字的长文拆分成十个 10 分钟的碎片进行阅读，系统会为您保管每一次停顿的锚点。

docs.title: 如何查看与使用 Timeline？
docs.text:
1. 展开时间线抽屉：
    • 在复习队列侧边栏中，选中并单击打开某篇已追踪的笔记。
    • 在该笔记的条目下方（或通过相关快捷操作），通常会展开一个 Timeline 抽屉区域。
  2. 解读历史记录：
    • 在抽屉中，您可以看到这篇笔记过去每一次的推进记录（Commit），包括当时的时间戳以及阅读到了该文档的什么位置（如 45%）。
  3. 恢复上下文位置：
    • 单击 Timeline 中较早的一条记录，主编辑区将尝试自动滚动到当时阅读的近似位置。这让您能迅速找回当时的语境。

docs.title: 触发记录的机制
docs.text:
请注意，Timeline 并不是实时的高频日志，它通常在您对该笔记进行明确的复习动作时生成记录。

  • 当您阅读完一部分内容，并通过命令或按钮对该笔记执行“推进（Review/Good/Hard 等）”或“延期”操作时，系统才会捕捉当前的滚动位置，并在 Timeline 中固化为一条新记录。

docs.title: 界面定制与设置
docs.text:
如果您觉得 Timeline 占用了过多的侧边栏空间，或者不需要记录滚动位置，可以前往插件设置页的 Notes 标签进行调整：

  • 显示滚动百分比（Show scroll percentage）：开启或关闭进度数值的显示。
  • 自动展开 Timeline（Auto-expand Timeline）：控制点击笔记时，时间线抽屉是否默认展开。

docs.title: 常见误区提示
docs.text:
• 期望 100% 的像素级精准定位：Timeline 的定位依赖于文档的结构与滚动比例。如果您在两次阅读之间，对该文档的原文结构进行了大规模的增删改写，旧记录对应的上下文位置可能会发生一定偏移，仅能作为近似参考。
  • 清理数据的风险：Timeline 的历史记录依赖于底层的独立数据文件（如 review_commits.json 等）。如果您在排障时随意删除了这些文件，该笔记的历史阅读上下文也将随之丢失。

相关章节导航：

  • 掌控笔记复习队列
  • 数据、同步与备份机制
  • 笔记复习总览
